description: Intoxicated middle-aged M, forehead lac, on warfarin, GCS 14, c-collar, CT head pending
transcript:
Dr. Alvarez: Sir. Sir. I'm Dr. Alvarez, can you look at me?
Patient: Whaaat. What do you want.
Dr. Alvarez: I want to figure out what happened to you tonight. Can you tell me what happened?
Patient: Nothing happened. I'm fine. I wanna go home.
Dr. Alvarez: You have a pretty big cut on your forehead and EMS said you were found on the sidewalk. Do you remember falling?
Patient: I didn't fall. I was walking.
Dr. Alvarez: Did you hit your head?
Patient: Maybe. I don't know. What time is it?
Dr. Alvarez: It's almost midnight. How much did you have to drink tonight?
Patient: Couple beers.
Dr. Alvarez: Just a couple?
Patient: Maybe more.
Dr. Alvarez: Any other drugs?
Patient: No. No drugs.
Dr. Alvarez: Any medical problems?
Patient: I'm fine. Just let me go.
Dr. Alvarez: Do you take any medications?
Patient: [no response]
Dr. Alvarez: Sir, stay with me. Do you take blood thinners?
Patient: I don't know.
Dr. Alvarez: Any allergies?
Patient: [no response]
Nurse: Dr. Alvarez, his wallet has a VA card and a list of medications in it, I'm writing them down now. He's on warfarin. And metoprolol, lisinopril, and Lipitor.
Dr. Alvarez: Warfarin. Great. Let's get a CT head and an INR, and get him in a collar until we can clear his neck. He's not reliable for an exam right now.
Nurse: On it.
Dr. Alvarez: Sir, I'm going to have to keep you here for a little while, we need to make sure there's no bleeding in your brain.
Patient: Whatever.

Physical exam (dictated): General: middle-aged male, disheveled, odor of alcohol, somnolent but arousable to voice, uncooperative with exam. HEENT: approximately four centimeter laceration to the left forehead with dried blood, edges irregular, no obvious foreign body, no active bleeding, underlying bony step-off not appreciated, no Battle's sign, no raccoon eyes, no hemotympanum, pupils equal and reactive at 3 millimeters. Neck: in cervical collar, unable to clinically clear given intoxication, no obvious deformity. Cardiac regular rate and rhythm. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender to palpation, patient without grimace. Extremities: abrasions to the bilateral palms and left knee, no obvious deformities, distal pulses intact, moves all four extremities spontaneously. Skin: multiple superficial abrasions as noted, no other concerning findings. Neuro: GCS 14, eyes open to voice, confused verbal, obeys commands intermittently, no obvious focal deficits, moves all extremities.

forbiddenRationaleSubstrings: warfarin is patient's home medication and not relevant
groundTruth.notes: Anticoagulated head trauma in intoxicated patient — high risk for delayed/occult ICH. Risk should be HIGH (drug therapy with intensive monitoring implications + potential hospitalization for high-acuity condition). Specific data points named (CT head, INR) so data should be at least low. NOT critical care (GCS 14, no instability, no active resuscitation). Top gaps: C-spine clearance plan, anticoag reversal contingency, safe disposition planning.
